title: Diagnostic Correlation of CSF Cytology, Culture, and Molecular Findings in Paediatric Pyogenic Meningitis: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Priyanshu Soni
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Paediatric pyogenic meningitis continues to be a major cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide, particularly in developing countries where early diagnosis and appropriate management are often hindered by limited laboratory resources. Accurate identification of the causative organism is critical for guiding timely and effective therapy. This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated the diagnostic correlation and performance of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) cytology, bacterial culture, and molecular assays, including polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and multiplex PCR, in children with pyogenic meningitis. Following the PRISMA 2020 guidelines, electronic databases including PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, and Google Scholar were searched up to August 2025 for studies assessing these diagnostic modalities in paediatric populations. Thirty-four studies comprising a total of 4,789 children met the inclusion criteria and were analyzed. Pooled sensitivity, specificity, and diagnostic odds ratio (DOR) were calculated using a random-effects model. The pooled sensitivity was 0.68 (95% CI: 0.61–0.74) for CSF cytology, 0.55 (95% CI: 0.47–0.63) for bacterial culture, and 0.89 (95% CI: 0.84–0.93) for molecular assays. Corresponding specificities were 0.82 (95% CI: 0.77–0.87), 0.97 (95% CI: 0.94–0.99), and 0.92 (95% CI: 0.89–0.95), respectively. The diagnostic odds ratio was highest for molecular techniques (78.2; 95% CI: 52.1–117.3), confirming their superior discriminative ability. Multiplex PCR assays demonstrated greater sensitivity than single-target PCR, and molecular tests identified pathogens in up to 50% of cases that were negative by cytology and culture. These results indicate that while cytology remains a valuable preliminary test and culture an indispensable reference standard for antibiotic susceptibility, molecular assays provide the most accurate and rapid diagnostic confirmation, especially in culture-negative or antibiotic-pretreated cases. An integrated diagnostic approach that combines cytology, culture, and molecular methods offers the highest diagnostic yield and should be adopted as the standard of care for paediatric bacterial meningitis to ensure early detection, appropriate therapy, and improved clinical outcomes.
